summary: Gruber responds to a New York Times investigation into Apple's iCloud compromises in China, arguing that while the security concerns are legitimate, the report fails to offer any plausible alternative. He frames Apple's choice as binary: comply with Chinese law and serve Chinese customers with the best privacy possible under those laws, or pull out entirely and abandon those users. He acknowledges the revenue motive but argues that Chinese iCloud users are likely better off with Apple's compromised service than without it. He also pushes back on the Times' implication that Apple removed 'Designed by Apple in California' from iPhones as political appeasement, arguing it was clearly a design decision applied across all text on iPhone backs.
key_points: Physical access to servers means game over for security, but Apple had no option other than compliance with Chinese law or total market withdrawal
The principled argument for pulling out of China ignores that Chinese users would lose access to what may be their most private technology option
Apple's real vulnerability is its irreplaceable Chinese manufacturing supply chain, which makes market withdrawal practically impossible

The NYT's claim that Apple dropped 'Designed by Apple in California' from iPhones to appease Chinese employees doesn't hold up — Apple systematically removed all text from iPhone backs as a design choice
Zuckerberg using Apple's China position to call them privacy hypocrites is convenient but doesn't change the calculus for Chinese users
money_quotes: Apple has no option other than to comply with Chinese law, or else stop selling products in the country.
You're living in a fantasy world if you think Apple taking a principled stand against these laws would have resulted in the Chinese government capitulating to Apple.
It's disingenuous to argue that Apple's compliance with Chinese law on data centers and App Store content is wrong or mistaken without offering up a plausible scenario for what else they could do.
What's more important: abstract ideals or the actual lives of actual people using these devices?

Even with the multiple significant compromises Apple has made to comply with Chinese law, it feels entirely possible that using Apple devices and iCloud is one of the most private things anyone outside government leadership can do in China.
Does it help Apple feel comfortable with the path they've chosen that it's the path that generates $40-50 billion in annual revenue? I'll bet it does.
Xi Jinping doesn't strike me as a 'No hard feelings' sort of guy.
Can you even imagine going into a meeting with Phil Schiller and Jony Ive and suggesting an iPhone case back design change based on this anecdote?
key_insight: Criticizing Apple's China compromises is easy, but no critic has offered a plausible alternative that would actually leave Chinese users better off.
